text: welcome to 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English.
zh: 欢迎收听BBC英语学习节目《六分钟英语》。

text: I'm Bekkah.
zh: 我是Bekkah。

text: And I'm Georgie.
zh: 我是Georgie。

text: Remember, you can find all this episode's vocabulary along with a transcript and worksheet on our website bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 别忘了，你可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到本期的词汇表、文字稿和练习题。

text: Now Bekkah, do you read a lot?
zh: Bekkah，你经常读书吗？

text: Hmm, I don't read often.
zh: 嗯，我读书不太多。

text: I just feel like I don't have the time, Georgie.
zh: 我总觉得没有时间，Georgie。

text: How about yourself?
zh: 你呢？

text: I would love to read more, but I don't read very much at the moment.
zh: 我很想多读一些书，但目前读得不多。

text: I read mostly before bed because I feel like it helps me relax and go to sleep.
zh: 我大多在睡前读书，因为我觉得这样能帮我放松，更容易入睡。

text: And today we're talking all about reading.
zh: 今天我们要聊聊阅读这个话题。

text: We'll be hearing from some experts about how reading can change our brains.
zh: 我们会听一些专家讲解阅读如何改变我们的大脑。

text: And as usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases.
zh: 像往常一样，我们也会学习一些实用的新词汇和短语。

text: Let's start with a quiz question.
zh: 我们先来做一道测验题。

text: The longest novel in the world is widely thought to be by French author Marcel Proust.
zh: 世界上公认最长的小说，被认为出自法国作家马塞尔·普鲁斯特之手。

text: A book which, when translated into English, means remembrance of things past.
zh: 这本书如果翻译成英文，书名意为《追忆似水年华》。

text: But how many words does the book contain?
zh: 但这本书到底有多少个单词呢？

text: Is it a 130 ,000, b 1 .3 million or c 13 million?
Okay, the longest novel in the world.
zh: 好的，世界上最长的小说。

text: I still think 13 million words sounds too many.
zh: 我还是觉得1300万这个数字听起来太多了。

text: So I'm going to go with B, 1 .3 million.
zh: 所以我选B，130万。

text: All right, we'll find out at the end of the program.
zh: 好，我们在节目结尾会揭晓答案。

text: Now, we might think of reading as like speaking.
zh: 我们可能会觉得阅读就像说话一样。

text: We're born with the potential to do it, and then we learn.
zh: 我们天生就具备说话的潜能，然后通过学习掌握它。

text: It's natural.
zh: 这是自然而然的。

text: If something is natural, it's something you were born with or that comes from nature.
zh: 如果某样东西是“natural（自然的）”，那就是说它是你与生俱来的，或者源自天性。

text: But Marianne Wolfe, author of the book Reader Come Home, says that this isn't true.
zh: 但《读者，回家》一书的作者玛丽安·沃尔夫说，事实并非如此。

text: We think of language as natural, and reading is written language, so it must be natural.
zh: 我们认为语言是自然的，而阅读就是书面语言，所以它也应该是自然的。

text: But it isn't.
zh: 但其实不是。

text: It isn't natural at all.
zh: 阅读根本不是自然而然的。

text: Scientific studies suggest that when we're born, our brains already have the networks that allow our eyes to see and our vocal cords to produce sounds, but not with the pathways we need to read.
zh: 科学研究表明，我们出生时，大脑中已经具备让眼睛看东西、让声带发出声音所需的神经网络，却没有阅读所需的神经通路。

text: Let's hear more from psychologist and neuroscientist Rebecca Gottlieb, speaking to the BBC World Service.
zh: 我们来听听心理学家兼神经科学家丽贝卡·戈特利布在接受BBC世界服务采访时怎么说。

text: From an evolutionary timescale, our brain hasn't had enough time to develop a dedicated reading brain.
zh: 从进化的时间尺度来看，我们的大脑还没有足够的时间演化出一个专门用于阅读的脑区。

text: And so to build a reading brain network, we co -op parts of the brain involved in vision and auditory processing and language and attention and and affect.
zh: 因此，为了构建阅读脑网络，我们借用了大脑中负责视觉、听觉处理、语言、注意力以及情感的部分。

text: Reading is really a whole brain process.
zh: 阅读其实是一个动用全脑的过程。

text: It involves activation in all four lobes of the cortex.
zh: 它涉及大脑皮层四个脑叶的激活。

text: The process of developing a reading brain alters everything from brain activity to brain structure and brain connectivity.
zh: 阅读脑的发展过程会改变从大脑活动到大脑结构，再到大脑连接方式的方方面面。

text: The power of deep reading is really fundamental to our humanity.
zh: 深度阅读的力量对我们的人性来说是根本性的。

text: When we read deeply, we change our brains and we change who we are.
zh: 当我们深入阅读时，我们改变了自己的大脑，也改变了我们自己。

text: Rebecca says that our brains haven't evolved to include a dedicated reading brain.
zh: 丽贝卡说，我们的大脑并没有演化出一个专门负责阅读的区域。

text: Dedicated here means designed and used for one particular purpose.
zh: 这里的“dedicated（专门的）”指的是专为某一特定目的而设计和使用的。

text: So, because we don't have a part of the brain designed specifically for reading, when we learn to read we co -opt other parts of the brain.
zh: 所以，因为大脑中没有专门用于阅读的区域，我们在学习阅读时，就会借用大脑的其他部分。

text: Co -opt here means to include someone or something, often against their will.
zh: 这里的“co-opt（借用/征用）”指的是把某人或某物纳入其中，通常并非出于其本意。

text: Right.
zh: 没错。

text: Learning to read means using lots of different parts of the brain that are designed for other things, and this changes our brain structure compared to someone who hasn't learned to read.
zh: 学习阅读意味着要动用大脑中许多本来是为其他功能设计的区域，这会改变我们的大脑结构，使之不同于从未学过阅读的人。

text: And the language we read also shapes our brain.
zh: 而我们所阅读的语言本身，也会塑造我们的大脑。

text: Chinese characters, for example, use symbols instead of letters of the alphabet to represent words and ideas.
zh: 比如说，汉字使用的是符号，而不是字母表中的字母，来表示词语和概念。

text: Research suggests that learning to read these symbols activates different areas of the brain to reading an alphabet -based system.
zh: 研究表明，学习识读这些符号所激活的脑区，与阅读以字母为基础的文字系统所激活的脑区不同。

text: Scientists studied a bilingual man who could read and speak Chinese and English.
zh: 科学家研究了一位能读写中文和英文的双语人士。

text: The man suffered a stroke which affected parts of his brain, including his ability to read Chinese.
zh: 这位男士曾中风，影响了他大脑的部分区域，包括他阅读中文的能力。

text: But amazingly, he was still able to read English.
zh: 但令人惊讶的是，他仍然能够阅读英文。

text: Marianne Wolfe explains more to the BBC World Service.
zh: 玛丽安·沃尔夫在BBC世界服务采访中进一步解释道。

text: It's a beautiful example of how the brain's circuit reflects the requirements environments of Chinese,
zh: 这是一个很好的例子，说明大脑的神经回路是如何反映中文这种语言环境所提出的要求的，

text: which inevitably means more visual memory and visual processing of those beautifully intricate symbols or characters.
zh: 而这必然意味着需要更多的视觉记忆，以及对那些精美繁复的符号或汉字进行视觉处理。

text: Marianne says that the brain circuit is shaped by learning to read Chinese.
zh: 玛丽安说，学习阅读中文塑造了大脑的神经回路。

text: A circuit is a system of connections.
zh: “circuit（回路）”指的是一种连接系统。

text: The visual qualities of Chinese symbols inevitably mean more visual areas of the brain are developed.
zh: 汉字的视觉特性必然意味着大脑中更多与视觉相关的区域得到发展。

text: Inevitably means in a way that cannot be stopped or avoided.
zh: “inevitably（不可避免地）”意味着一种无法阻止或避免的方式。

text: Mary -Anne describes the symbolic Chinese characters as beautifully intricate.
zh: 玛丽安将这些象形的汉字描述为“beautifully intricate（精美繁复）”。

text: If something is intricate, it has lots of detail.
zh: 如果某样东西是“intricate（复杂精细的）”，那就是说它包含很多细节。

text: And something which also has lots of detail, or certainly lots of words, I asked you, Georgie, how many words are in Marcel Proust's Remembrance of Things Past?
zh: 说到细节多、字数也肯定多的东西，我之前问过你，Georgie，马塞尔·普鲁斯特的《追忆似水年华》一共有多少个单词？

text: I said 1 .3 million.
zh: 我说的是130万。

text: And you were correct!
zh: 你答对了！

text: Yay!
zh: 太棒了！

text: The book also contains lots of very long sentences, including one with over 900 words.
zh: 这本书还包含许多非常长的句子，其中有一句竟然超过900个单词。

text: One sentence with 900 words, that is a lot.
zh: 一句话900个单词，这真是不少啊。

text: OK, it's time to recap the language we learned during this programme, starting with natural, which describes something you were born with or that comes from nature.
zh: 好了，是时候来回顾一下本期节目中学到的词汇了，首先是“natural”，形容你与生俱来或源于天性的东西。

text: Dedicated can describe something that is designed and used for one particular purpose.
zh: “dedicated”可以形容专为某一特定目的而设计和使用的事物。

text: If you co -opt someone or something, you involve them, sometimes against their will.
zh: 如果你“co-opt”某人或某物，就是把他们卷入其中，有时并非出于自愿。

text: A circuit is a system of connections, for example, in the brain.
zh: “circuit”指的是一种连接系统，比如大脑中的神经回路。

text: Inevitably means in a way that cannot be stopped or avoided.
zh: “inevitably”意味着以一种无法阻止或避免的方式。

text: And intricate describes something which has lots of detail.
zh: “intricate”形容包含很多细节的事物。

text: That's it for this episode of 6 Minute English.
zh: 本期《六分钟英语》就到这里。

text: Test what you've learnt with the worksheet on our website bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 快去我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上，用练习题检测一下你的学习成果吧。

text: Thanks for joining us.
zh: 感谢大家的收听。

text: Goodbye.
zh: 再见。

text: Bye.
zh: 拜拜。
